flashcards.back: Multiplexing is a technique that combines multiple signals from different sources into a single shared transmission medium to efficiently utilize available bandwidth. It allows multiple users to share a high-capacity channel simultaneously.

flashcards.front: What is Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)? Derive the condition for successful FDM.
flashcards.back: FDM assigns a unique frequency band to each signal. The required bandwidth B_total = B₁ + B₂ + ... + Bₙ + 2Σ(guard bands), where B_total must be ≤ bandwidth of transmission medium. Guard bands prevent interference between adjacent channels.

flashcards.front: Calculate the number of channels in a Synchronous TDM system with frame duration 1ms and each time slot is 50μs.
flashcards.back: Number of channels n = Frame duration / Slot duration = 1ms / 50μs = 1000μs / 50μs = 20 channels. Each channel transmits one sample per frame in synchronous TDM.

flashcards.front: Explain Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM) and its types in optical fiber communication.
flashcards.back: WDM combines multiple light signals at different wavelengths onto a single fiber. Coarse WDM (C-WDM) uses 2-8 channels with 20nm spacing; Dense WDM (D-WDM) uses 40+ channels with 0.8nm or 100GHz spacing, enabling terabit transmission.

flashcards.front: What is Code Division Multiplexing (CDM) / CDMA? How does orthogonal coding work?
flashcards.back: CDMA assigns a unique orthogonal code (e.g., Walsh code) to each user. All users transmit simultaneously using spread spectrum. The receiver uses correlation to extract the desired signal: S_recovered = S_transmitted × Code, where Code₁ × Code₂ = 0 for orthogonality.

flashcards.front: Compare FDM and TDM based on bandwidth efficiency, hardware complexity, and synchronization requirements.
flashcards.back: FDM: continuous bandwidth allocation, requires analog filters, no synchronization needed, suffers from intermodulation distortion. TDM: discrete time slots, requires digital circuitry, needs strict synchronization, higher bandwidth efficiency, easier to implement digitally.

flashcards.front: State the Shannon-Hartley Theorem and explain its relevance to multiplexing capacity.
flashcards.back: Theorem: C = B × log₂(1 + S/N), where C = max capacity (bps), B = bandwidth (Hz), S/N = signal-to-noise ratio. Multiplexing increases effective bandwidth B, directly increasing channel capacity C. This defines the theoretical limit for any multiplexing technique.

flashcards.front: List practical applications of each multiplexing technique in modern communication systems.
flashcards.back: FDM: Radio broadcasting, TV transmission, satellite communication. TDM: Digital telephone systems (PCM), GSM air interface. WDM: Long-haul fiber optic networks, data center connections. CDMA: Mobile phones (3G), GPS, military spread-spectrum communications.
